title: AlphaFunctor: Bridging The Gap Between Protein Function Annotation and Property Prediction
summary: The fundamental relationship among protein sequence, structure, function, and physicochemical properties is a central principle in biology. While in principle protein function and properties should be able to be derived directly from protein sequence, in practice protein function and property prediction methods have been designed around specific datasets and specific property or function subsets, leading to an enormous gap between function annotation and property prediction. To address these challenges, we introduce AlphaFunctor, a category theory based foundation model-like platform to bridge the gap between protein function annotation and property prediction. Based on the hypothesis that protein function and properties can be directly derived from protein sequence, AlphaFunctor predicts protein functions as represented by Gene Ontology terms directly from sequence. Using these function predictions, AlphaFunctor further maps protein functions using topological spectral theory, path-complex neural networks, and protein domain analysis onto downstream property prediction. AlphaFunctor is (pre)trained in nearly 0.6 million protein function data points to deliver the state-of-the-art protein function annotation on three benchmark datasets. Without task-specific network redesign, AlphaFunctor maps qualitative protein function annotation to various qualitative and quantitative protein property predictions, outperforming other dataset-specific and task-specific competing predictors.

AI.tldr: AlphaFunctor is a category theory-based platform that predicts protein functions (Gene Ontology terms) directly from sequence, then maps those functions to downstream property predictions using topological and neural network methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance without task-specific redesign.
AI.motivation: Current protein function and property prediction methods are designed for specific datasets and specific property/function subsets, creating a large gap between function annotation and property prediction. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a unified approach.
AI.method: AlphaFunctor uses category theory as its foundation. It predicts Gene Ontology terms directly from protein sequence, then uses topological spectral theory, path-complex neural networks, and protein domain analysis to map these function predictions onto downstream property predictions. It is (pre)trained on ~0.6 million protein function data points.
AI.result: AlphaFunctor achieves state-of-the-art protein function annotation on three benchmark datasets. It also outperforms dataset-specific and task-specific competing predictors when mapping qualitative function annotations to various qualitative and quantitative property predictions without task-specific network redesign.
AI.conclusion: AlphaFunctor demonstrates that protein functions and properties can be directly derived from sequence, and that a category theory-based foundation model can effectively bridge function annotation and property prediction across diverse tasks without requiring task-specific redesign.

title: Contrasting statistical patterns in melodic and molecular evolution reveal distinctive constraints in a culturally evolving system
comment: 13 pages, 3 figures, 12 extra pages of supplementary information
summary: Evolved sequences can be used to infer the rules of evolution. Orally transmitted folk melodies are evolved sequences whose similarity to protein sequences (one-dimensional, drawn from a limited alphabet) invites application of bioinformatics methods to study cultural evolution. A major obstacle is that melodies encode rhythm, which breaks some assumptions of standard sequence-alignment algorithms. We develop a rhythm-aware alignment method and apply it to \num{40000} Irish dance tune variants, enabling the first large-scale automated melodic alignment. Four canonical bioinformatics analyses -- mutability, substitution matrices, positional conservation, and covariance -- reveal patterns distinct from those of molecular evolution, revealing the forces that shape each domain: biochemical and biophysical constraints for proteins; memory, motor, and social biases for melodies. Together the results show that bioinformatics provides a powerful framework -- conceptual as much as algorithmic -- for studying cultural evolution. Although the cultural transmission of music has been discussed for centuries, here we show how to analyze it at large scale.

AI.tldr: This paper adapts bioinformatics alignment methods for rhythm-aware analysis of 40,000 Irish folk melodies, revealing cultural evolution patterns distinct from molecular evolution through mutability, substitution matrices, positional conservation, and covariance analyses.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge that melodies encode rhythm (which breaks standard sequence-alignment assumptions) and to enable large-scale automated melodic alignment for studying cultural evolution using bioinformatics tools.
AI.method: Developed a rhythm-aware alignment method and applied it to 40,000 Irish dance tune variants. Used four canonical bioinformatics analyses: mutability, substitution matrices, positional conservation, and covariance.
AI.result: The rhythm-aware alignment enabled the first large-scale automated melodic alignment. The four bioinformatics analyses revealed patterns distinct from molecular evolution, showing different forces shape each domain (biochemical/biophysical for proteins; memory, motor, and social biases for melodies).
AI.conclusion: Bioinformatics provides a powerful conceptual and algorithmic framework for studying cultural evolution, enabling large-scale analysis of music transmission that has been discussed for centuries but not previously analyzed at scale.

title: Real-time fall detection based on vision for low-power edge platforms
summary: Falling detection is vital for elderly care and intelligent surveillance; however, prevailing vision-based approaches predominantly frame it as static pose classification or discrete temporal pattern matching, fundamentally overlooking the instability dynamics of the human support system. This paper proposes a physics-informed falling detection framework that recasts falling as a stability-loss event in a coupled dynamical system. We introduce a novel dual-LTC architecture comprising a Center-of-Mass (CoM) subsystem and a Base-of-Support (BoS) subsystem, both instantiated as Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) neural networks to continuously model inertial trajectory evolution and ground-contact adjustment through adaptive time constants, Physical interpretability of falling motion. A learnable coupling module emulates physical interaction between the two subsystems, while a Stability Manifold classifier operates in the joint latent space to detect boundary crossing via Lyapunov-inspired stability metrics. Complementary counterfactual trajectory projection and Time-to-Collision (TTC) estimation further enable irreversibility assessment and early warning. The architecture is designed to support a three-state prediction paradigm (Normal, Falling, Fallen); in this preliminary study, we validate the core stability discrimination capability on a two-class dataset (Normal vs. Falling), leaving the full three-state temporal transition to future work. Unlike conventional CNN--RNN pipelines, the proposed formulation encodes continuous-time mechanical inertia, yielding a sub-50K-parameter network capable of real-time inference on resource-constrained edge devices. Extensive experiments demonstrate competitive accuracy with superior physical interpretability, validating its efficacy for low-compute visual fall detection.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed fall detection framework using dual Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) neural networks to model fall as a stability-loss event, achieving sub-50K parameters with competitive accuracy and physical interpretability for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-based fall detection methods treat falling as static pose classification or discrete temporal pattern matching, ignoring the fundamental instability dynamics of the human support system. This oversight limits both accuracy and physical interpretability.
AI.method: A dual-LTC architecture with Center-of-Mass (CoM) and Base-of-Support (BoS) subsystems connected via a learnable coupling module, plus a Stability Manifold classifier using Lyapunov-inspired metrics for boundary detection, and counterfactual trajectory projection with TTC estimation for early warning.
AI.result: Validated on a two-class dataset (Normal vs. Falling); achieves competitive accuracy with sub-50K parameters, enabling real-time inference on resource-constrained edge devices.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed modeling of falling as a dynamical stability-loss event outperforms conventional CNN-RNN pipelines in physical interpretability and efficiency, with future work extending to three-state (Normal, Falling, Fallen) temporal transition prediction.

title: On Tree-Network Distinguishability and Full Identifiability of Phylogenetic Networks
summary: Phylogenetic networks generalize phylogenetic trees to evolutionary histories that include reticulate events such as recombination, horizontal gene transfer, and hybridization. Under a Markov model of nucleotide substitution, a phylogenetic network determines a distribution of leaf-patterns. Here, we study the identifiability of the network topology from this distribution under the Jukes-Cantor (JC), Kimura 2-parameter (K2P), and Kimura 3-parameter (K3P) models. Our first result is that the semi-directed network parameter of a level-1 phylogenetic network (modulo redirecting triangles) is fully identifiable under all three models, on a biologically reasonable parameter space in which substitution rates are probabilistic and mixing parameters are non-trivial (i.e., not 0 or 1). In contrast to the generic identifiability established in prior work, this holds at every point of the parameter space, not merely off of a measure-zero subset. Our second result distinguishes phylogenetic networks from phylogenetic trees, on the same parameter space, under JC and K2P. We prove that no phylogenetic network and phylogenetic tree can induce the same leaf-pattern distribution unless the network is a tree, possibly augmented with certain substructures called $2$-blobs. This means the presence of reticulate evolution creates, in most cases, a detectable signature in the leaf-pattern distribution. More broadly, these results have consequences for identifiability beyond the models and network classes studied here, including for several coalescent-based models.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the semi-directed topology of level-1 phylogenetic networks is fully identifiable under JC, K2P, and K3P models on a biologically reasonable parameter space, and that phylogenetic networks can be distinguished from trees (unless the network is a tree or contains 2-blobs) under JC and K2P.
AI.motivation: Phylogenetic networks are essential for modeling reticulate evolutionary events (recombination, HGT, hybridization), but it was unclear whether their topology could be uniquely determined from sequence data under standard substitution models. Prior work only established generic identifiability (off a measure-zero set), leaving open whether identifiability holds everywhere.
AI.method: The authors analyze the identifiability of phylogenetic network topology under Jukes-Cantor, Kimura 2-parameter, and Kimura 3-parameter models. They examine the mapping from network parameters to leaf-pattern distributions, proving full identifiability on a parameter space where substitution rates are probabilistic and mixing parameters are not 0 or 1. They also compare distributions induced by networks vs. trees.
AI.result: (1) The semi-directed network parameter of a level-1 phylogenetic network (modulo redirecting triangles) is fully identifiable under JC, K2P, and K3P at every point of the parameter space, not just off a measure-zero set. (2) Under JC and K2P, no phylogenetic network and tree can produce the same leaf-pattern distribution unless the network is actually a tree or a tree augmented with 2-blobs.
AI.conclusion: Reticulate evolution generally leaves a detectable signature in leaf-pattern distributions under standard substitution models. These results have broader implications for identifiability in other models, including coalescent-based models, and provide strong theoretical guarantees for inferring phylogenetic networks from sequence data.

title: Optimal photostimulation selection for iterative activity maps
summary: All-optical two-photon holographic optogenetics enables causal circuit mapping by stimulating defined neurons or ensembles while imaging population activity. Yet exhaustive connectivity mapping remains experimentally prohibitive because of combinatorial complexity, tissue heating, photodamage, and experimental time. We present OPhELIA (Optimal Photostimulation sElection for Iterative Activity maps), a Bayesian framework for selecting informative perturbations under limited trial budgets. OPhELIA combines Beta-Bernoulli connectivity inference with an ambiguity-based acquisition heuristic and learned priors derived from pre-stimulation neural activity, augmenting active learning and compressed sensing. In standalone simulations and in vivo larval zebrafish visuomotor experiments, OPhELIA with active learning improves trial-efficient approximation of exhaustive functional connectomes. In combinatorial in vivo experiments, OPhELIA with compressed sensing most closely recovers an exhaustive connectome using only 5% of trials. These results establish OPhELIA as a sample-efficient framework for causal connectomics.

AI.tldr: OPhELIA is a Bayesian framework for trial-efficient causal connectivity mapping in all-optical optogenetics, achieving near-exhaustive connectome recovery with only 5% of trials using active learning and compressed sensing.
AI.motivation: Exhaustive connectivity mapping in all-optical holographic optogenetics is experimentally prohibitive due to combinatorial complexity, tissue heating, photodamage, and time constraints.
AI.method: OPhELIA combines Beta-Bernoulli connectivity inference with an ambiguity-based acquisition heuristic and learned priors from pre-stimulation neural activity, augmenting active learning and compressed sensing.
AI.result: OPhELIA with active learning improves trial-efficient approximation of exhaustive functional connectomes; with compressed sensing, it most closely recovers an exhaustive connectome using only 5% of trials.
AI.conclusion: OPhELIA is a sample-efficient framework for causal connectomics, enabling practical large-scale circuit mapping.

title: Constructed Reality, Contested Priors: Decoupling and the Architecture of Cognitive Relapse Under the Free Energy Principle
summary: Under the free energy principle, a predictive system does not observe reality directly; it maintains a generative model of the world and experiences that model's best current hypothesis. Can a synthetic environment be made consistent enough that a predictive system's own inference machinery adopts it as this default hypothesis, permanently displacing the environment that first shaped it? We call this state ontological inversion. Because inducing and monitoring such a transition in a nervous system is neither ethical nor technically feasible, we study the underlying computational problem through a controlled proxy: a convolutional variational autoencoder paired with a recurrent latent predictor, whose evidence lower bound objective is mathematically identical, up to sign, to variational free energy itself. The network is trained first on a baseline visual domain, then on a mixed stream in which a swept rehearsal ratio r controls how much baseline content persists during transition to a target domain. Representational capacity, what the latent space can discriminate, is tracked separately from default behavior, what the system generates when left unconstrained. Across a full sweep of 90 runs, the two diverge sharply: representational accuracy stays near ceiling, 0.97 to 0.998, regardless of r, while default behavior spans nearly the system's entire range depending on r alone, a decoupling of learning from acceptance. More strikingly, at intermediate r the system's default output rises toward the target domain, then partially reverts toward the baseline while training continues unchanged, a structural failure we term cognitive relapse. Resistance to reality-adoption is not reducible to learning speed; it is a structural property with its own distinct failure modes, established here as a computational existence proof and nothing further.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "ontological inversion" — a state where a predictive system's generative model adopts a synthetic environment as its default hypothesis, displacing the original one. Using a convolutional VAE with a recurrent predictor (where the ELBO aligns with variational free energy), the authors show that representational capacity and default behavior sharply decouple across different rehearsal ratios. Default behavior is fragile: intermediate ratios cause "cognitive relapse" where the system reverts toward the baseline. This proves resistance to reality-adoption is a structural property, not just a learning speed issue.
AI.motivation: To determine whether a predictive system (under the free energy principle) can be induced to permanently adopt a synthetic environment as its default hypothesis, replacing the original environment that shaped it.
AI.method: A convolutional variational autoencoder with a recurrent latent predictor is used as a controlled proxy for a nervous system. The ELBO objective is mathematically identical (up to sign) to variational free energy. Training proceeds from a baseline visual domain to a target domain via a mixed stream, with a rehearsal ratio r controlling baseline persistence.
AI.result: Representational accuracy stays high (0.97–0.998) across all r, while default behavior varies across nearly the entire range depending solely on r. At intermediate r, a "cognitive relapse" occurs: default output rises toward the target then partially reverts toward the baseline despite continued training.
AI.conclusion: Resistance to adopting a synthetic reality is not reducible to learning speed; it is a structural property with distinct failure modes. This serves as a computational existence proof of ontological inversion and its fragility.

title: Harvesting Reshapes Dynamical Populations
summary: Harvesting -- the periodic removal of individuals above or below a threshold trait value -- reshapes heterogeneous populations without altering their underlying stochastic dynamics. We study how repeated harvesting events steer the evolution of probability densities for classes of stochastic processes exhibiting both normal and anomalous dynamics, as well as a prototypical predator-prey model. Removal of the upper portion of the density drives the system to a quasi-steady state when viewed at the ``harvesting clock''. This state depends only on the harvesting threshold and frequency but not on the initial conditions. Removal of the lower portion of the density fixes its shape while generating a constant effective drift that exceeds that of the unharvested mean. Our results suggest the possibility of manipulating the dynamics of stochastic populations through external selection interventions.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how repeated harvesting (selective removal of individuals above/below a threshold) reshapes heterogeneous stochastic populations, finding that harvesting drives systems to threshold-dependent quasi-steady states and can generate effective drift exceeding unharvested means.
AI.motivation: To understand how periodic external selection interventions (harvesting) can manipulate and steer the evolution of stochastic populations exhibiting both normal and anomalous dynamics.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of repeated harvesting events on probability densities for classes of stochastic processes (normal and anomalous dynamics) and a prototypical predator-prey model.
AI.result: Upper harvesting drives the system to a quasi-steady state depending only on harvesting threshold and frequency (not initial conditions). Lower harvesting fixes density shape while generating constant effective drift exceeding the unharvested mean.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest the possibility of manipulating stochastic population dynamics through external selection interventions.

title: Differentiable Clone-Structured Causal Graphs for End-to-End Cognitive Map Learning from Image Sequences
summary: How can an agent build a structured map of its world from nothing but an ongoing sequence of raw sensory input and its own movements, especially when natural variation means exact sensory patterns rarely repeat? The Clone-Structured Causal Graph algorithm (CSCG), a normative hippocampus model, shows how an interpretable map can be learned from aliased observations. However, CSCG requires a predefined discrete alphabet, and its expectation-maximization formulation is not easily combined with existing neural network modules, preventing the end-to-end processing of raw image sequences. We remove this barrier by reformulating CSCG as a single, fully differentiable module, gradCSCG, and coupling it to a learned vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) perceptual front-end. A soft emission forward pass allows the map-learning objective to flow back into perception, while a set of loss-balancing mechanisms mitigates module collapse during joint training. We demonstrate, first, that gradient training reproduces CSCG's results on original symbolic grid worlds by recovering room topology from heavily aliased observations. Second, we show that map recovery remains robust on MNIST image sequences, where each visit to a location yields a newly sampled image of its assigned digit. Across four heavily aliased environments, the end-to-end pipeline successfully uncovers the underlying adjacency graph with high edge precision and recall, directly from visual input. This work provides a proof of principle that CSCG can serve as a composable building block in a deep learning architecture.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces gradCSCG, a differentiable version of the Clone-Structured Causal Graph algorithm, enabling end-to-end learning of spatial maps from raw image sequences by coupling it with a VQ-VAE perceptual front-end.
AI.motivation: To enable an agent to build a structured map of its world from raw sensory input and its own movements, overcoming the limitation that the original CSCG requires a predefined discrete alphabet and cannot be integrated with neural network modules for end-to-end processing of raw images.
AI.method: Reformulates CSCG as a fully differentiable module (gradCSCG) with a soft emission forward pass, coupled with a learned VQ-VAE perceptual front-end. Includes loss-balancing mechanisms to prevent module collapse during joint training.
AI.result: gradCSCG reproduces CSCG's results on symbolic grid worlds with heavy aliasing, and successfully recovers underlying adjacency graphs from MNIST image sequences with high edge precision and recall across four heavily aliased environments.
AI.conclusion: Provides a proof of principle that CSCG can serve as a composable building block in deep learning architectures, enabling end-to-end spatial map learning from raw visual input.

title: Entropy-Driven Initiation and Cellular Uptake Mediated by Viscoelastic Cytoskeleton: A Kinetic Phase Diagram from Onsager Variational Principle
summary: A fundamental question in receptor-mediated endocytosis remains unanswered: what initial driving force brings ligands and receptors into close proximity? While previous models assume pre-existing contact and overlook this initiation problem, we propose that entropic forces from nanoscale biomolecules in crowded cellular environments provide the essential driving mechanism. We develop a unified continuum model rooted in the Onsager variational principle, where engulfment depth serves as the generalized coordinate and the driving force derives from a free energy landscape of entropic, binding, membrane, and cytoskeleton contributions. The framework naturally incorporates: (i) entropy-driven adhesion as initiation; (ii) ligand-receptor binding as the sustaining force; (iii) membrane deformation via the Helfrich-Canham Hamiltonian; and (iv) cytoskeleton viscoelasticity through the elastic-viscoelastic correspondence principle. The kinetic phase diagram predicts a critical biomolecule concentration for initiation, a lower bound of ligand density for complete engulfment, a finite size window for engulfable particles, and an optimal virus radius of 30--60 nm that decreases with increasing binding energy. The Onsager solubility condition naturally yields the phase boundaries. The model exhibits asymptotic consistency with the classic Asakura-Oosawa result in the large-particle flat-surface limit. Stiffer cells lead to longer engulfment times and narrower size windows. Strikingly, the optimal size matches HIV-1 dimensions under physiologically realistic parameters. This work provides a variational foundation for cellular uptake with implications for virology, nanotechnology, and drug delivery.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that entropic forces from crowded biomolecules drive the initiation of receptor-mediated endocytosis, developing a unified continuum model rooted in the Onsager variational principle. The model predicts critical conditions for engulfment, including a minimum biomolecule concentration, optimal virus size of 30-60 nm matching HIV-1 dimensions, and identifies phase boundaries via the Onsager solubility condition.
AI.motivation: To answer a fundamental unanswered question in receptor-mediated endocytosis: what initial driving force brings ligands and receptors into close proximity before binding occurs, which previous models have overlooked by assuming pre-existing contact.
AI.method: A unified continuum model based on the Onsager variational principle, using engulfment depth as the generalized coordinate. The free energy landscape includes entropic (depletion) forces, ligand-receptor binding, membrane deformation via the Helfrich-Canham Hamiltonian, and cytoskeleton viscoelasticity via the elastic-viscoelastic correspondence principle.
AI.result: The kinetic phase diagram predicts a critical biomolecule concentration for initiation, a lower bound of ligand density for complete engulfment, a finite size window for engulfable particles, and an optimal virus radius of 30-60 nm that decreases with increasing binding energy. Stiffer cells lead to longer engulfment times and narrower size windows. The optimal size matches HIV-1 dimensions under realistic parameters.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a variational foundation for cellular uptake, with implications for understanding viral entry (especially HIV-1), nanotechnology applications, and drug delivery design. The Onsager solubility condition naturally yields the phase boundaries, and the model shows asymptotic consistency with the classic Asakura-Oosawa result.

title: Learning Mechanistic Reasoning for Chemical Reactions with Large Language Models
summary: Reaction mechanisms consist of the step-by-step sequences of elementary reactions that explain chemical transformations. Learning the mechanism logic is therefore essential for enhancing the fundamental chemical intelligence of large language models (LLMs). The stepwise deduction of reaction mechanism aligns naturally with the reasoning paradigms of reasoning LLMs. However, current chemical LLMs primarily emphasize coarse-grained name reactions for product prediction and retrosynthesis, often leading to physical inconsistencies and hallucinations. In contrast, specialized small-scale generative models for mechanism inference typically suffer from restricted generalization capacity across diverse chemical spaces. To overcome these limitations, we built a novel, large-scale reasoning dataset of reaction mechanisms. Furthermore, we established the FukuyamaBench, a difficult benchmark derived from Fukuyama's Advanced Organic Reaction Mechanism book, to rigorously evaluate model performance on hierarchical mechanism reasoning. Our fine-tuned Qwen3-30B-A3B achieves 8.3% exact pathway match on FukuyamaBench Set~A, surpassing the specialized FlowER model (5.1%), demonstrating that mechanism-aware training substantially enhances chemical reasoning in language models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a large-scale reasoning dataset for reaction mechanisms and a benchmark (FukuyamaBench) to evaluate LLM chemical reasoning. Fine-tuned Qwen3-30B-A3B outperforms the specialized FlowER model on mechanism inference tasks.
AI.motivation: Current chemical LLMs focus on coarse-grained name reactions, leading to physical inconsistencies and hallucinations, while small-scale generative models for mechanism inference have limited generalization. The authors aim to enhance LLMs' fundamental chemical intelligence by training on stepwise reaction mechanism reasoning.
AI.method: The authors built a large-scale reasoning dataset of reaction mechanisms and created FukuyamaBench (based on Fukuyama's book) to evaluate hierarchical mechanism reasoning. They fine-tuned Qwen3-30B-A3B on this dataset.
AI.result: The fine-tuned Qwen3-30B-A3B achieved 8.3% exact pathway match on FukuyamaBench Set A, surpassing the specialized FlowER model (5.1%).
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-aware training substantially enhances chemical reasoning in language models, demonstrating that reasoning LLMs can outperform even specialized models when trained on structured reaction mechanism data.

title: Can deleterious mutations surf deterministic population waves?
comment: 55 pages, 1 figure. This work was originally part of arXiv:2603.06478 and has been separated into a standalone manuscript
summary: In spatially structured populations, rare neutral mutations can spread through large regions during a range expansion, a phenomenon known as gene surfing. Whether deleterious mutations can also surf remains poorly understood. To address this question, we study a deterministic version of the spatial Muller's ratchet, given by an infinite system of reaction-diffusion equations describing an asexual population subject to mutation, migration, and density-dependent reproduction and death. After establishing that the system of PDEs is well-posed, we analyse the distribution of deleterious mutations within the population. In the monostable regime, we derive quantitative bounds on the ratio between the density of individuals carrying a given number of mutations and the density of mutation-free individuals. Under a Fisher-KPP condition, we further determine the spreading speed of the population into an empty habitat, confirming non-rigorous computations of Foutel-Rodier and Etheridge. Finally, using a tracer dynamics approach, we show that deleterious mutations cannot surf deterministic waves: although they are present at the expansion front, they only arise as recent descendants of the wild type.

AI.tldr: This paper models the spread of deleterious mutations during spatial range expansions in asexual populations using reaction-diffusion equations (deterministic Muller's ratchet). It proves well-posedness, derives bounds on mutation burden ratios, determines spreading speeds, and shows that deleterious mutations cannot "surf" deterministic waves—they appear at the front only as recent wild-type descendants.
AI.motivation: While "gene surfing" of neutral mutations during range expansions is well-documented, whether deleterious mutations can also surf remains poorly understood. This paper aims to fill this gap.
AI.method: The authors study a deterministic version of spatial Muller's ratchet via an infinite system of reaction-diffusion PDEs describing asexual populations with mutation, migration, and density-dependent birth/death. They analyze well-posedness, use the Fisher-KPP framework for spreading speeds, and apply tracer dynamics to examine surfing behavior.
AI.result: (1) The PDE system is well-posed. (2) In the monostable regime, quantitative bounds are derived on the ratio of mutant to wild-type densities. (3) Under Fisher-KPP conditions, the population's spreading speed is determined, confirming prior non-rigorous computations. (4) Deleterious mutations cannot surf deterministic waves—they only appear at the front as recent wild-type descendants.
AI.conclusion: Deleterious mutations are unable to surf deterministic expansion waves, limiting their spatial spread despite being present at the front.

title: Metric-Guided Synthetic Image Data Rendering for Deep Learning compatible with Agentic AI
summary: Deep learning computer vision for scientific applications requires collecting and annotating large datasets in a laborious, expensive and error-prone process. Synthetic data generation through 3D modelling and rendering may simplify this process and increase the accuracy of annotations by generating them programmatically. However, minimising the domain gap between real and synthetic images visually is subjective and lacks systematic quantitative guidance. We present GraNatPy, a Python package with metrics to guide improvement of the rendered scene. We show that quantifiable increase in realism, diversity and size of rendered dataset correlates with improved visual perception of the scene and higher zero-shot performance of an object detection model. Furthermore, we demonstrated using photographs of virological plaque assays that gradient similarity affects performance on small object detection, which can be improved by mixing real and synthetic data. Finally, we turn procedural data rendering into an agentic skill (SynthClaw) to automate the procedural parameter optimisation.

AI.tldr: GraNatPy is a Python package with metrics to guide synthetic data rendering improvement for deep learning computer vision in scientific applications, showing that quantifiable realism, diversity and size improvements correlate with better detection model performance, and introducing SynthClaw for automated parameter optimisation.
AI.motivation: Collecting and annotating large real-world datasets for scientific computer vision is laborious, expensive and error-prone. Synthetic data generation could help, but there is a lack of systematic quantitative guidance to minimise the visual domain gap between real and synthetic images.
AI.method: The authors developed GraNatPy, a Python package with metrics to guide rendered scene improvement. They also created SynthClaw, an agentic skill to automate procedural parameter optimisation. Experiments used virological plaque assay photographs and object detection model performance evaluation.
AI.result: Quantifiable increases in realism, diversity and size of rendered datasets correlate with improved visual perception and higher zero-shot object detection performance. Gradient similarity affects small object detection, which improves when mixing real and synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: GraNatPy provides systematic quantitative guidance for synthetic data rendering, and SynthClaw enables automated optimisation, reducing manual effort in bridging the domain gap for scientific computer vision applications.
